description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child uses place value and math rules to add and subtract numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare two three-digit numbers based on meanings of the hundreds, tens, and ones digits, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two three-digit numbers by looking at the hundreds, tens, and ones digits and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Read and write numbers to 1000 using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form.
description_plain: Your child can read and write numbers up to 1000 in regular form, word form, and expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Count within 1000; skip-count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.
description_plain: Your child can count numbers up to 1000 and skip-count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.

subject: Math
description: The numbers 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine hundreds (and 0 tens and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 100, 200, 300, and so on mean one, two, three, etc., hundreds with zero tens and ones.

subject: Math
description: 100 can be thought of as a bundle of ten tens — called a "hundred."
description_plain: Your child knows that 100 is the same as a bundle of ten tens, called a 'hundred.'

subject: Math
description: Understand that the three digits of a three-digit number represent amounts of hundreds, tens, and ones; e.g., 706 equals 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones. Understand the following as special cases:
description_plain: Your child understands that a three-digit number shows how many hundreds, tens, and ones it has, like 706 means 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones.

subject: Math
description: Understand place value.
description_plain: Understand place value.

subject: Math
description: Use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rectangular arrays with up to 5 rows and up to 5 columns; write an equation to express the total as a sum of equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rows and columns (up to 5 rows and 5 columns) and write an equation showing the total as equal groups added together.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a group of objects (up to 20) has an odd or even number of members, e.g., by pairing objects or counting them by 2s; write an equation to express an even number as a sum of two equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a group of up to 20 objects has an odd or even number by pairing them or counting by twos, and write an equation showing an even number as two equal groups added together.

subject: Math
description: Work with equal groups of objects to gain foundations for multiplication.
description_plain: Your child works with equal groups of objects to start learning multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 20 using mental strategies. By end of Grade 2, know from memory all sums of two one-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers within 20 in their head and knows all sums of two one-digit numbers by memory.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 20.
description_plain: Add and subtract within 20.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve one- and two-step word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve one- and two-step word problems using addition and subtraction within 100, with unknown numbers anywhere in the problem, using drawings or equations.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can show and solve problems that use addition and subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns when solving problems repeatedly.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can find and use the way things are organized or structured to help solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their thinking clearly and listen to others' ideas to decide if they make sense.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems both in general ideas and with real numbers.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand problems and keep trying until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Describe qualitatively the functional relationship between two quantities by analyzing a graph (e.g., where the function is increasing or decreasing, linear or nonlinear). Sketch a graph that exhibits the qualitative features of a function that has been described verbally.
description_plain: Your child can look at a graph and describe how two things are related, like when one goes up or down, and can draw a graph based on a description.

subject: Math
description: Construct a function to model a linear relationship between two quantities. Determine the rate of change and initial value of the function from a description of a relationship or from two (x, y) values, including reading these from a table or from a graph. Interpret the rate of change and initial value of a linear function in terms of the situation it models, and in terms of its graph or a table of values.
description_plain: Your child can create a math rule (function) to show a straight-line relationship between two things, find how fast one changes compared to the other, and explain what these numbers mean using tables or graphs.

subject: Math
description: Use functions to model relationships between quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use functions to show how two quantities are connected.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the equation y = mx + b as defining a linear function, whose graph is a straight line; give examples of functions that are not linear.
description_plain: Your child understands that the equation y = mx + b makes a straight line on a graph and can give examples of functions that don’t make straight lines.

subject: Math
description: Compare properties of two functions each represented in a different way (algebraically, graphically, numerically in tables, or by verbal descriptions).
description_plain: Your child can compare two functions shown in different ways, like with equations, graphs, tables, or words.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a function is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. The graph of a function is the set of ordered pairs consisting of an input and the corresponding output.
description_plain: Your child understands that a function is a rule that gives exactly one output for each input, and can see this as pairs of inputs and outputs on a graph.

subject: Math
description: Define, evaluate, and compare functions.
description_plain: Your child can define what a function is, find its value for given inputs, and compare different functions.

subject: Math
description: Understand that patterns of association can also be seen in bivariate categorical data by displaying frequencies and relative frequencies in a two-way table. Construct and interpret a two-way table summarizing data on two categorical variables collected from the same subjects. Use relative frequencies calculated for rows or columns to describe possible association between the two variables.
description_plain: Your child can make and read two-way tables that show how two categories relate, using counts and percentages to describe their connection.

subject: Math
description: Use the equation of a linear model to solve problems in the context of bivariate measurement data, interpreting the slope and intercept.
description_plain: Your child can use a straight-line equation to solve problems with two related measurements and explain what the slope and starting point mean.

subject: Math
description: Know that straight lines are widely used to model relationships between two quantitative variables. For scatter plots that suggest a linear association, informally fit a straight line, and informally assess the model fit by judging the closeness of the data points to the line.
description_plain: Your child knows that straight lines often show relationships between two numbers and can draw a line that fits data points to see how well it matches.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate measurement data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities. Describe patterns such as clustering, outliers, positive or negative association, linear association, and nonlinear association.
description_plain: Your child can make and understand scatter plots to find patterns between two quantities, like groups, unusual points, or types of relationships.

subject: Math
description: Investigate patterns of association in bivariate data.
description_plain: Your child explores how two sets of data relate to each other.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems leading to two linear equations in two variables.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems that involve two linear equations with two variables.

subject: Math
description: Solve systems of two linear equations in two variables algebraically, and estimate solutions by graphing the equations. Solve simple cases by inspection.
description_plain: Your child can solve pairs of linear equations using algebra, estimate answers by graphing, and solve simple cases by looking.

subject: Math
description: Understand that solutions to a system of two linear equations in two variables correspond to points of intersection of their graphs, because points of intersection satisfy both equations simultaneously.
description_plain: Your child understands that the solution to two linear equations is where their graphs cross, because that point fits both equations.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
description_plain: Your child can analyze and solve pairs of linear equations that happen at the same time.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations with rational number coefficients, including equations whose solutions require expanding expressions using the distributive property and collecting like terms.
description_plain: Your child can solve linear equations with fractions and decimals, including those needing to expand expressions and combine like terms.

subject: Math
description: Give examples of linear equations in one variable with one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solutions. Show which of these possibilities is the case by successively transforming the given equation into simpler forms, until an equivalent equation of the form x = a, a = a, or a = b results (where a and b are different numbers).
description_plain: Your child can give examples of one-variable linear equations that have one solution, many solutions, or no solution, and show which by simplifying the equation.

subject: Math
description: Solve linear equations in one variable.
description_plain: Your child can solve linear equations with one variable.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and solve linear equations and pairs of simultaneous linear equations.
description_plain: Your child can analyze and solve both single linear equations and pairs of linear equations.

subject: Math
description: Use similar triangles to explain why the slope m is the same between any two distinct points on a non-vertical line in the coordinate plane; derive the equation y = mx for a line through the origin and the equation y = mx + b for a line intercepting the vertical axis at b.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the slope is the same between any two points on a line and can write the equation of a line through the origin or with a vertical intercept.

subject: Math
description: Graph proportional relationships, interpreting the unit rate as the slope of the graph. Compare two different proportional relationships represented in different ways.
description_plain: Your child can graph proportional relationships, understand the unit rate as the slope, and compare different proportional relationships shown in various ways.

subject: Math
description: Understand the connections between proportional relationships, lines, and linear equations.
description_plain: Your child understands how proportional relationships connect to lines and linear equations.

subject: Math
description: Perform operations with numbers expressed in scientific notation, including problems where both decimal and scientific notation are used. Use scientific notation and choose units of appropriate size for measurements of very large or very small quantities (e.g., use millimeters per year for seafloor spreading). Interpret scientific notation that has been generated by technology.
description_plain: Your child can do math with numbers in scientific notation, choose suitable units for very big or small measurements, and understand scientific notation from technology.

subject: Math
description: Use numbers expressed in the form of a single digit times an integer power of 10 to estimate very large or very small quantities, and to express how many times as much one is than the other.
description_plain: Your child can use numbers written as a digit times a power of 10 to estimate very large or small amounts and compare how many times bigger one is than another.

subject: Math
description: Use square root and cube root symbols to represent solutions to equations of the form x² = p and x³ = p, where p is a positive rational number. Evaluate square roots of small perfect squares and cube roots of small perfect cubes. Know that √2 is irrational.
description_plain: Your child can use square root and cube root symbols to solve equations like x² = p or x³ = p, find roots of small perfect squares and cubes, and knows that √2 cannot be written as a simple fraction.

subject: Math
description: Know and apply the properties of integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child knows and uses the rules for integer exponents to rewrite numerical expressions in different but equal ways.

subject: Math
description: Work with radicals and integer exponents.
description_plain: Your child works with roots and integer exponents.

subject: Math
description: Use rational approximations of irrational numbers to compare the size of irrational numbers, locate them approximately on a number line diagram, and estimate the value of expressions (e.g., π²).
description_plain: Your child can use close rational numbers to compare and estimate irrational numbers and place them roughly on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Know that numbers that are not rational are called irrational. Understand informally that every number has a decimal expansion; for rational numbers show that the decimal expansion repeats eventually, and convert a decimal expansion which repeats eventually into a rational number.
description_plain: Your child knows that irrational numbers are not fractions, understands that all numbers have decimal forms, and can turn repeating decimals into fractions.

subject: Math
description: Know that there are numbers that are not rational, and approximate them by rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child knows some numbers are not fractions and can approximate them using fractions.

subject: Math
description: Know the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child knows the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres and can use them to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems involving volume of cylinders, cones, and spheres.
description_plain: Your child can solve real-life and math problems involving the volume of cylinders, cones, and spheres.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points in a coordinate system.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points on a coordinate grid.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to determine unknown side lengths in right triangles in real-world and mathematical problems in two and three dimensions.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find missing side lengths in right triangles in both two- and three-dimensional problems.

subject: Math
description: Explain a proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse.
description_plain: Your child can explain the proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and its reverse.

subject: Math
description: Understand and apply the Pythagorean Theorem.
description_plain: Your child understands and can use the Pythagorean Theorem.

subject: Math
description: Use informal arguments to establish facts about the angle sum and exterior angle of triangles, about the angles created when parallel lines are cut by a transversal, and the angle-angle criterion for similarity of triangles.
description_plain: Your child can use simple reasoning to explain facts about triangle angles, angles made by parallel lines cut by a transversal, and how to tell if triangles are similar.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a two-dimensional figure is similar to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, translations, and dilations; given two similar two-dimensional figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the similarity between them.
description_plain: Your child understands that one 2D shape is similar to another if it can be made by rotating, flipping, sliding, and resizing the first, and can describe how to do this.

subject: Math
description: Describe the effect of dilations, translations, rotations, and reflections on two-dimensional figures using coordinates.
description_plain: Your child can describe how rotating, sliding, flipping, and resizing shapes changes their coordinates.

subject: Math
description: Understand that a two-dimensional figure is congruent to another if the second can be obtained from the first by a sequence of rotations, reflections, and translations; given two congruent figures, describe a sequence that exhibits the congruence between them.
description_plain: Your child understands that two 2D shapes are congruent if one can be made by rotating, flipping, and sliding the other, and can explain how.

subject: Math
description: Parallel lines are taken to parallel lines.
description_plain: Your child knows that parallel lines stay parallel after transformations like rotations, reflections, and translations.

subject: Math
description: Angles are taken to angles of the same measure.
description_plain: Your child knows that angles keep their size after rotations, reflections, and translations.

subject: Math
description: Lines are taken to lines, and line segments to line segments of the same length.
description_plain: Your child knows that lines stay lines and line segments keep their length after rotations, reflections, and translations.

subject: Math
description: Verify experimentally the properties of rotations, reflections, and translations:
description_plain: Your child can test and confirm the properties of rotations, reflections, and translations through hands-on activities.

subject: Math
description: Understand congruence and similarity using physical models, transparencies, or geometry software.
description_plain: Your child can use physical tools or computer programs to understand when shapes are the same size and shape or just similar.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns when solving problems repeatedly.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can find and use the way things are organized or structured to help solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their thinking clearly and listen to others' ideas to decide if they make sense.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems both in general ideas and with real numbers.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand problems and keep trying until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two and four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, fourths, and quarters, and use the phrases half of, fourth of, and quarter of. Describe the whole as two of, or four of the shares. Understand for these examples that decomposing into more equal shares creates smaller shares.
description_plain: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into halves and quarters, use words like halves and fourths to describe them, and understand that more pieces mean smaller parts.

subject: Math
description: Compose two-dimensional shapes (rectangles, squares, trapezoids, triangles, half-circles, and quarter-circles) or three-dimensional shapes (cubes, right rectangular prisms, right circular cones, and right circular cylinders) to create a composite shape, and compose new shapes from the composite shape.
description_plain: Your child can put together flat shapes or solid shapes to make new shapes and then create even more shapes from those.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish between defining attributes (e.g., triangles are closed and three-sided) versus non-defining attributes (e.g., color, orientation, overall size); build and draw shapes to possess defining attributes.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between important shape features like having three sides for triangles and less important features like color or size, and can make shapes with the important features.

subject: Math
description: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
description_plain: Your child can think about shapes and their important features.

subject: Math
description: Organize, represent, and interpret data with up to three categories; ask and answer questions about the total number of data points, how many in each category, and how many more or less are in one category than in another.
description_plain: Your child can organize and understand data with up to three groups, and ask and answer questions about how many items are in each group and compare the groups.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time in hours and half-hours using analog and digital clocks.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write time to the hour and half-hour using both analog and digital clocks.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time.
description_plain: Tell and write time.

subject: Math
description: Express the length of an object as a whole number of length units, by laying multiple copies of a shorter object (the length unit) end to end; understand that the length measurement of an object is the number of same-size length units that span it with no gaps or overlaps.
description_plain: Your child can measure how long something is by lining up smaller objects end to end without gaps or overlaps and counting how many fit.

subject: Math
description: Order three objects by length; compare the lengths of two objects indirectly by using a third object.
description_plain: Your child can put three objects in order from shortest to longest and compare lengths by using a third object.

subject: Math
description: Measure lengths indirectly and by iterating length units.
description_plain: Your child can measure lengths by using repeated units and by comparing lengths indirectly.

subject: Math
description: Subtract multiples of 10 in the range 10-90 from multiples of 10 in the range 10-90 (positive or zero differences), using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can subtract multiples of 10 from each other using drawings or strategies based on place value, explain their method, and connect it to written subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Given a two-digit number, mentally find 10 more or 10 less than the number, without having to count; explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can quickly find 10 more or 10 less than any two-digit number in their head and explain how they figured it out.

subject: Math
description: Add within 100, including adding a two-digit number and a one-digit number, and adding a two-digit number and a multiple of 10, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used. Understand that in adding two-digit numbers, one adds tens and tens, ones and ones; and sometimes it is necessary to compose a ten.
description_plain: Your child can add numbers up to 100, including adding a two-digit number with a one-digit number or a multiple of 10, using drawings or objects and explaining their thinking.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use place value and math rules to add and subtract numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare two two-digit numbers based on meanings of the tens and ones digits, recording the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, and <.
description_plain: Your child can compare two two-digit numbers by looking at their tens and ones digits and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: The numbers 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine tens (and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 10, 20, 30, and so on mean one, two, three, etc. groups of ten and zero ones.

subject: Math
description: The numbers from 11 to 19 are composed of a ten and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
description_plain: Your child knows that numbers from 11 to 19 are made of one ten and some extra ones.

subject: Math
description: 10 can be thought of as a bundle of ten ones — called a "ten."
description_plain: Your child understands that 10 is a group of ten ones, called a 'ten.'

subject: Math
description: Understand that the two digits of a two-digit number represent amounts of tens and ones. Understand the following as special cases:
description_plain: Your child understands that the two digits in a two-digit number show how many tens and ones there are.

subject: Math
description: Understand place value.
description_plain: Understand place value.

subject: Math
description: Count to 120, starting at any number less than 120. In this range, read and write numerals and represent a number of objects with a written numeral.
description_plain: Your child can count to 120 starting from any number below 120, read and write numbers in this range, and match numbers to groups of objects.

subject: Math
description: Extend the counting sequence.
description_plain: Extend the counting sequence.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unknown whole number in an addition or subtraction equation relating three whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can find the missing number in addition or subtraction problems with three whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand the meaning of the equal sign, and determine if equations involving addition and subtraction are true or false.
description_plain: Your child understands what the equal sign means and can tell if addition or subtraction equations are true or false.

subject: Math
description: Work with addition and subtraction equations.
description_plain: Your child works with addition and subtraction equations.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 20, demonstrating fluency for addition and subtraction within 10. Use strategies such as counting on; making ten (e.g., 8 + 6 = 8 + 2 + 4 = 10 + 4 = 14); decomposing a number leading to a ten (e.g., 13 - 4 = 13 - 3 - 1 = 10 - 1 = 9); using the relationship between addition and subtraction (e.g., knowing that 8 + 4 = 12, one knows 12 - 8 = 4); and creating equivalent but easier or known sums (e.g., adding 6 + 7 by creating the known equivalent 6 + 6 + 1 = 12 + 1 = 13).
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 20 using strategies like counting on, making ten, breaking numbers apart, and using what they know about addition and subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Relate counting to addition and subtraction (e.g., by counting on 2 to add 2).
description_plain: Your child connects counting with adding and subtracting, like counting on to add.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 20.
description_plain: Add and subtract within 20.

subject: Math
description: Understand subtraction as an unknown-addend problem.
description_plain: Your child understands subtraction as finding the missing addend in an addition problem.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child uses math rules to help add and subtract.

subject: Math
description: Understand and apply properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child understands and uses math rules and the connection between addition and subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems that call for addition of three whole numbers whose sum is less than or equal to 20, e.g., by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add three whole numbers with a total of 20 or less, using objects, drawings, or equations with a missing number.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 20 to solve word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions, e.g., by using objects, drawings, and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child solves word problems with addition and subtraction within 20, using objects, drawings, or equations with a missing number.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can show and solve problems using addition and subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns when solving problems repeatedly.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can find and use the way things are organized or structured to help solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their thinking clearly and listen to others' ideas to decide if they make sense.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems both in general ideas and with real numbers.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand problems and keep trying until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Compose simple shapes to form larger shapes.
description_plain: Your child can put simple shapes together to make bigger shapes.

subject: Math
description: Model shapes in the world by building shapes from components (e.g., sticks and clay balls) and drawing shapes.
description_plain: Your child can build shapes using objects like sticks and clay and draw shapes they see around them.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and compare two- and three-dimensional shapes, in different sizes and orientations, using informal language to describe their similarities, differences, parts (e.g., number of sides and vertices/"corners") and other attributes (e.g., having sides of equal length).
description_plain: Your child can look at flat and solid shapes of different sizes and describe how they are alike and different, including parts like sides and corners.

subject: Math
description: Analyze, compare, create, and compose shapes.
description_plain: Your child can look at, compare, create, and put together different shapes.

subject: Math
description: Identify shapes as two-dimensional (lying in a plane, "flat") or three-dimensional ("solid").
description_plain: Your child understands the difference between flat shapes (2D) and solid shapes (3D).

subject: Math
description: Correctly name shapes regardless of their orientations or overall size.
description_plain: Your child can name shapes correctly no matter how they are turned or how big they are.

subject: Math
description: Describe objects in the environment using names of shapes, and describe the relative positions of these objects using terms such as above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to.
description_plain: Your child can name shapes they see and describe where things are using words like above, below, beside, in front of, behind, and next to.

subject: Math
description: Identify and describe shapes (squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres).
description_plain: Your child can recognize and talk about shapes like squares, circles, triangles, rectangles, hexagons, cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres.

subject: Math
description: Classify objects into given categories; count the numbers of objects in each category and sort the categories by count.
description_plain: Your child can sort objects into groups, count how many are in each group, and put the groups in order by size.

subject: Math
description: Classify objects and count the number of objects in each category.
description_plain: Your child can sort objects into categories and count how many are in each category.

subject: Math
description: Directly compare two objects with a measurable attribute in common, to see which object has "more of"/"less of" the attribute, and describe the difference.
description_plain: Your child can compare two objects to see which one has more or less of something like length or weight and explain the difference.

subject: Math
description: Describe measurable attributes of objects, such as length or weight. Describe several measurable attributes of a single object.
description_plain: Your child can describe things about objects that can be measured, like how long or heavy they are, and talk about several of these features for one object.

subject: Math
description: Describe and compare measurable attributes.
description_plain: Your child can describe and compare things about objects that can be measured.

subject: Math
description: Compose and decompose numbers from 11 to 19 into ten ones and some further ones, e.g., by using objects or drawings, and record each composition or decomposition by a drawing or equation (e.g., 18 = 10 + 8); understand that these numbers are composed of ten ones and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
description_plain: Your child can break apart and put together numbers from 11 to 19 using groups of ten and extra ones, and show this with drawings or equations.

subject: Math
description: Work with numbers 11—19 to gain foundations for place value.
description_plain: Your child works with numbers 11 to 19 to understand how place value works.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 5.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 5.

subject: Math
description: For any number from 1 to 9, find the number that makes 10 when added to the given number, e.g., by using objects or drawings, and record the answer with a drawing or equation.
description_plain: Your child can find the number that adds to another number from 1 to 9 to make 10, using objects or drawings, and write it down.

subject: Math
description: Decompose numbers less than or equal to 10 into pairs in more than one way, e.g., by using objects or drawings, and record each decomposition by a drawing or equation (e.g., 5 = 2 + 3 and 5 = 4 + 1).
description_plain: Your child can break numbers up to 10 into pairs in different ways and show this with drawings or equations.

subject: Math
description: Solve addition and subtraction word problems, and add and subtract within 10, e.g., by using objects or drawings to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve simple addition and subtraction story problems using objects or drawings.

subject: Math
description: Represent addition and subtraction with objects, fingers, mental images, drawings, sounds (e.g., claps), acting out situations, verbal explanations, expressions, or equations.
description_plain: Your child can show addition and subtraction using objects, fingers, pictures, sounds, acting, talking, or writing.

subject: Math
description: Understand addition as putting together and adding to, and understand subtraction as taking apart and taking from.
description_plain: Your child understands that addition means putting things together and subtraction means taking things apart or away.

subject: Math
description: Compare two numbers between 1 and 10 presented as written numerals.
description_plain: Your child can compare two numbers from 1 to 10 written as numerals to see which is bigger or smaller.

subject: Math
description: Identify whether the number of objects in one group is greater than, less than, or equal to the number of objects in another group, e.g., by using matching and counting strategies.
description_plain: Your child can tell if one group has more, less, or the same number of objects as another group by matching or counting.

subject: Math
description: Compare numbers.
description_plain: Compare numbers.

subject: Math
description: Count to answer "how many?" questions about as many as 20 things arranged in a line, a rectangular array, or a circle, or as many as 10 things in a scattered configuration; given a number from 1—20, count out that many objects.
description_plain: Your child can count up to 20 things arranged in a line, rectangle, or circle, or up to 10 things scattered, and can count out a given number of objects up to 20.

subject: Math
description: Understand that each successive number name refers to a quantity that is one larger.
description_plain: Your child understands that each number name you say when counting means one more than the number before.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the last number name said tells the number of objects counted. The number of objects is the same regardless of their arrangement or the order in which they were counted.
description_plain: Your child knows that the last number said when counting tells how many objects there are, no matter how they are arranged or counted.

subject: Math
description: When counting objects, say the number names in the standard order, pairing each object with one and only one number name and each number name with one and only one object.
description_plain: Your child counts objects by saying number names in order, matching one number to each object and one object to each number.

subject: Math
description: Understand the relationship between numbers and quantities; connect counting to cardinality.
description_plain: Your child understands how numbers and amounts are connected and that counting tells how many things there are.

subject: Math
description: Count to tell the number of objects.
description_plain: Your child can count to find out how many objects there are.

subject: Math
description: Write numbers from 0 to 20. Represent a number of objects with a written numeral 0-20 (with 0 representing a count of no objects).
description_plain: Your child can write numbers from 0 to 20 and use these numbers to show how many objects there are, including zero for none.

subject: Math
description: Count forward beginning from a given number within the known sequence (instead of having to begin at 1).
description_plain: Your child can count forward starting from any number they know, not just from 1.

subject: Math
description: Count to 100 by ones and by tens.
description_plain: Your child can count to 100 by ones and by tens.

subject: Math
description: Know number names and the count sequence.
description_plain: Your child knows number names and the order of counting.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns when solving problems repeatedly.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can find and use the way things are organized or structured to help solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their thinking clearly and listen to others' ideas to decide if they make sense.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems both in general ideas and with real numbers.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand problems and keep trying until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to hundredths by reasoning about their size. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two decimals refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual model.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimals to the hundredths place by thinking about their size, making sure the decimals refer to the same whole, and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Use decimal notation for fractions with denominators 10 or 100.
description_plain: Your child can write fractions with denominators of 10 or 100 as decimals.

subject: Math
description: Express a fraction with denominator 10 as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100, and use this technique to add two fractions with respective denominators 10 and 100.
description_plain: Your child can change a fraction with denominator 10 into an equivalent fraction with denominator 100 and use this to add fractions with denominators 10 and 100.

subject: Math
description: Understand decimal notation for fractions, and compare decimal fractions.
description_plain: Your child understands how to write fractions as decimals and compare decimal fractions.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving multiplication of a fraction by a whole number, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that multiply a fraction by a whole number using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Understand a multiple of a/b as a multiple of 1/b, and use this understanding to multiply a fraction by a whole number.
description_plain: Your child understands that multiplying a fraction a/b by a whole number is like multiplying 1/b by that number and then using this to multiply fractions by whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b as a multiple of 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction a/b means a groups of 1/b parts.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction by a whole number.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a fraction by a whole number by building on what they know about multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole and having like denominators, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that add or subtract fractions with the same denominator using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract mixed numbers with like denominators, e.g., by replacing each mixed number with an equivalent fraction, and/or by using properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract mixed numbers with the same denominator by changing them into improper fractions or using properties of addition and subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with the same denominator in more than one way, recording each decomposition by an equation. Justify decompositions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.
description_plain: Your child can break a fraction into a sum of smaller fractions with the same denominator in different ways and explain their thinking using pictures.

subject: Math
description: Understand addition and subtraction of fractions as joining and separating parts referring to the same whole.
description_plain: Your child understands that adding and subtracting fractions means putting parts together or taking parts apart from the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b with a > 1 as a sum of fractions 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction with numerator greater than 1 is the same as adding several unit fractions together.

subject: Math
description: Build fractions from unit fractions by applying and extending previous understandings of operations on whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can build fractions by putting together unit fractions and use what they know about whole numbers to work with fractions.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions with different numerators and different denominators, e.g., by creating common denominators or numerators, or by comparing to a benchmark fraction such as 1/2. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.
description_plain: Your child can compare two fractions with different numerators and denominators by finding common denominators or numerators, or by comparing to 1/2, and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.

subject: Math
description: Explain why a fraction a/b is equivalent to a fraction (n × a)/(n × b) by using visual fraction models, with attention to how the number and size of the parts differ even though the two fractions themselves are the same size. Use this principle to recognize and generate equivalent fractions.
description_plain: Your child can explain why multiplying the numerator and denominator of a fraction by the same number makes an equivalent fraction using pictures, and use this to find equivalent fractions.

subject: Math
description: Extend understanding of fraction equivalence and ordering.
description_plain: Your child understands that some fractions are equal and can put fractions in order from smallest to largest.

subject: Math
description: Recognize a line of symmetry for a two-dimensional figure as a line across the figure such that the figure can be folded along the line into matching parts. Identify line-symmetric figures and draw lines of symmetry.
description_plain: Your child can find lines of symmetry in flat shapes by folding them to see if the parts match, and can draw these lines.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures based on the presence or absence of parallel or perpendicular lines, or the presence or absence of angles of a specified size. Recognize right triangles as a category, and identify right triangles.
description_plain: Your child can sort flat shapes by whether they have parallel or perpendicular lines or certain angle sizes, and can recognize right triangles.

subject: Math
description: Draw points, lines, line segments, rays, angles (right, acute, obtuse), and perpendicular and parallel lines. Identify these in two-dimensional figures.
description_plain: Your child can draw points, lines, line segments, rays, different types of angles, and parallel or perpendicular lines, and find these in shapes.

subject: Math
description: Draw and identify lines and angles, and classify shapes by properties of their lines and angles.
description_plain: Your child can draw and recognize lines and angles, and group shapes based on their lines and angles.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angle measure as additive. When an angle is decomposed into non-overlapping parts, the angle measure of the whole is the sum of the angle measures of the parts. Solve addition and subtraction problems to find unknown angles on a diagram in real world and mathematical problems, e.g., by using an equation with a symbol for the unknown angle measure.
description_plain: Your child understands that the size of an angle is the sum of its parts and can solve problems to find missing angles using addition and subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Measure angles in whole-number degrees using a protractor. Sketch angles of specified measure.
description_plain: Your child can measure angles in whole degrees using a protractor and draw angles with a given size.

subject: Math
description: An angle that turns through n one-degree angles is said to have an angle measure of n degrees.
description_plain: Your child knows that an angle made by turning through n one-degree steps has a measure of n degrees.

subject: Math
description: An angle is measured with reference to a circle with its center at the common endpoint of the rays, by considering the fraction of the circular arc between the points where the two rays intersect the circle. An angle that turns through 1/360 of a circle is called a "one-degree angle," and can be used to measure angles.
description_plain: Your child understands that angles are measured by how much of a circle they cover, with one degree being 1/360 of a full circle.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angles as geometric shapes that are formed wherever two rays share a common endpoint, and understand concepts of angle measurement:
description_plain: Your child knows that angles are shapes made by two rays sharing a point and understands how to measure them.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of angle and measure angles.
description_plain: Your child understands what angles are and how to measure them.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (1/2, 1/4, 1/8). Solve problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions by using information presented in line plots.
description_plain: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements using fractions like 1/2, 1/4, or 1/8 and solve addition and subtraction problems using the plot.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Apply the area and perimeter formulas for rectangles in real world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the area and perimeter of rectangles in real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations to solve word problems involving distances, intervals of time, liquid volumes, masses of objects, and money, including problems involving simple fractions or decimals, and problems that require expressing measurements given in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Represent measurement quantities using diagrams such as number line diagrams that feature a measurement scale.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with measurements like distance, time, volume, weight, and money, including fractions and decimals, and can show measurements on number lines.

subject: Math
description: Know relative sizes of measurement units within one system of units including km, m, cm; kg, g; lb, oz.; l, ml; hr, min, sec. Within a single system of measurement, express measurements in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Record measurement equivalents in a two column table.
description_plain: Your child knows how big different measurement units are within one system (like kilometers, meters, centimeters) and can change measurements from bigger units to smaller units and record these conversions in a table.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving measurement and conversion of measurements from a larger unit to a smaller unit.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems that involve measuring and changing measurements from bigger units to smaller units.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients and remainders with up to four-digit dividends and one-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can divide numbers up to four digits by one-digit numbers using place value and multiplication/division facts, and explain their work with equations or pictures.

subject: Math
description: Multiply a whole number of up to four digits by a one-digit whole number, and multiply two two-digit numbers, using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a number up to four digits by a one-digit number and multiply two two-digit numbers using place value and properties of multiplication, and explain their work with equations or pictures.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract multi-digit whole numbers using the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract large whole numbers using the standard method.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child uses place value and math rules to add, subtract, multiply, and divide multi-digit numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round multi-digit whole numbers to any place.
description_plain: Your child can round multi-digit whole numbers to any place value.

subject: Math
description: Read and write multi-digit whole numbers using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form. Compare two multi-digit numbers based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can read and write large whole numbers in digits, words, and expanded form, and compare two numbers using >, =, or < based on their digits.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents in the place to its right.
description_plain: Your child understands that in a multi-digit number, a digit in one place is worth ten times what it is in the place to its right.

subject: Math
description: Generalize place value understanding for multi-digit whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands place value for large whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Generate a number or shape pattern that follows a given rule. Identify apparent features of the pattern that were not explicit in the rule itself.
description_plain: Your child can create number or shape patterns that follow a rule and notice features in the pattern that the rule doesn't directly say.

subject: Math
description: Generate and analyze patterns.
description_plain: Your child can make and study patterns.

subject: Math
description: Find all factor pairs for a whole number in the range 1—100. Recognize that a whole number is a multiple of each of its factors. Determine whether a given whole number in the range 1—100 is a multiple of a given one-digit number. Determine whether a given whole number in the range 1—100 is prime or composite.
description_plain: Your child can find all factor pairs of numbers from 1 to 100, know that numbers are multiples of their factors, decide if a number is a multiple of a one-digit number, and tell if a number is prime or composite.

subject: Math
description: Gain familiarity with factors and multiples.
description_plain: Your child understands factors and multiples.

subject: Math
description: Solve multistep word problems posed with whole numbers and having whole-number answers using the four operations, including problems in which remainders must be interpreted. Represent these problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems with several steps using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division, including problems with remainders, write equations with letters for unknowns, and check if answers make sense by estimating.

subject: Math
description: Multiply or divide to solve word problems involving multiplicative comparison, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem, distinguishing multiplicative comparison from additive comparison.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems comparing amounts by multiplying or dividing, using drawings and equations with symbols for unknown numbers, and tell the difference between multiplying and adding comparisons.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison, e.g., interpret 35 = 5 × 7 as a statement that 35 is 5 times as many as 7 and 7 times as many as 5. Represent verbal statements of multiplicative comparisons as multiplication equations.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication equations as comparisons, like seeing 35 = 5 × 7 as 35 is 5 times 7, and can write multiplication equations from words about comparisons.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations with whole numbers to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can use addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division with whole numbers to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice and explain patterns when solving problems repeatedly.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can find and use the way things are organized or structured to help solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Attend to precision.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Model with mathematics.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their thinking clearly and listen to others' ideas to decide if they make sense.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about math problems both in general ideas and with real numbers.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand problems and keep trying until they find a solution.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world problems involving division of unit fractions by non-zero whole numbers and division of whole numbers by unit fractions, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve real problems dividing unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a whole number by a unit fraction, and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child can understand and calculate what happens when you divide a whole number by a unit fraction.

subject: Math
description: Interpret division of a unit fraction by a non-zero whole number, and compute such quotients.
description_plain: Your child can understand and calculate what happens when you divide a unit fraction by a whole number.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of division to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions.
description_plain: Your child can divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions, building on what they already know about division.

subject: Math
description: Solve real world problems involving multiplication of fractions and mixed numbers, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve real problems multiplying fractions and mixed numbers using pictures or equations.

subject: Math
description: Explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction greater than 1 results in a product greater than the given number (recognizing multiplication by whole numbers greater than 1 as a familiar case); explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction less than 1 results in a product smaller than the given number; and relating the principle of fraction equivalence a/b = (n×a)/(n×b) to the effect of multiplying a/b by 1.
description_plain: Your child can explain why multiplying by a fraction bigger than 1 makes a number bigger, and multiplying by a fraction less than 1 makes it smaller, and understand multiplying fractions by 1 keeps them the same.

subject: Math
description: Comparing the size of a product to the size of one factor on the basis of the size of the other factor, without performing the indicated multiplication.
description_plain: Your child can compare the size of a multiplication result to one factor by looking at the other factor without actually multiplying.

subject: Math
description: Interpret multiplication as scaling (resizing), by:
description_plain: Your child understands multiplication as resizing or scaling.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths by tiling it with unit squares of the appropriate unit fraction side lengths, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths. Multiply fractional side lengths to find areas of rectangles, and represent fraction products as rectangular areas.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of rectangles with fractional side lengths by counting small squares and by multiplying the side lengths.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the product (a/b) × q as a parts of a partition of q into b equal parts; equivalently, as the result of a sequence of operations a × q ÷ b.
description_plain: Your child understands multiplying a fraction by a whole number as taking parts of that whole number or by multiplying and dividing in steps.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction or whole number by a fraction.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a fraction by another fraction or by a whole number.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a fraction as division of the numerator by the denominator (a/b = a ÷ b). Solve word problems involving division of whole numbers leading to answers in the form of fractions or mixed numbers, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child understands a fraction as dividing the top number by the bottom number and can solve word problems using this idea.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication and division to multiply and divide fractions.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide fractions and whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole, including cases of unlike denominators, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem. Use benchmark fractions and number sense of fractions to estimate mentally and assess the reasonableness of answers.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems adding and subtracting fractions with different denominators using pictures or equations and can estimate if answers make sense.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract fractions with unlike denominators (including mixed numbers) by replacing given fractions with equivalent fractions in such a way as to produce an equivalent sum or difference of fractions with like denominators.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract fractions with different denominators by changing them to have the same denominator first.

subject: Math
description: Use equivalent fractions as a strategy to add and subtract fractions.
description_plain: Your child uses equivalent fractions to help add and subtract fractions.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures in a hierarchy based on properties.
description_plain: Your child can sort flat shapes into groups based on their properties, like sides and angles.

subject: Math
description: Understand that attributes belonging to a category of two-dimensional figures also belong to all subcategories of that category.
description_plain: Your child understands that if a shape belongs to a big group, it also has the properties of smaller groups inside it.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures into categories based on their properties.
description_plain: Your child can put flat shapes into categories by looking at their features.

subject: Math
description: Represent real world and mathematical problems by graphing points in the first quadrant of the coordinate plane, and interpret coordinate values of points in the context of the situation.
description_plain: Your child can plot points on a graph in the first quadrant and explain what the points mean in real situations.

subject: Math
description: Use a pair of perpendicular number lines, called axes, to define a coordinate system, with the intersection of the lines (the origin) arranged to coincide with the 0 on each line and a given point in the plane located by using an ordered pair of numbers, called its coordinates. Understand that the first number indicates how far to travel from the origin in the direction of one axis, and the second number indicates how far to travel in the direction of the second axis, with the convention that the names of the two axes and the coordinates correspond (e.g., x-axis and x-coordinate, y-axis and y-coordinate).
description_plain: Your child understands how to use two number lines that cross at zero to find points using pairs of numbers called coordinates.

subject: Math
description: Graph points on the coordinate plane to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can graph points on a coordinate plane to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as additive. Find volumes of solid figures composed of two non-overlapping right rectangular prisms by adding the volumes of the non-overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real world problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of solid shapes made by joining two rectangular boxes by adding their volumes.

subject: Math
description: Apply the formulas V = l × w × h and V = b × h for rectangular prisms to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with whole-number edge lengths in the context of solving real world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the volume of rectangular boxes with whole number sides in real problems.

subject: Math
description: Find the volume of a right rectangular prism with whole-number side lengths by packing it with unit cubes, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths, equivalently by multiplying the height by the area of the base. Represent threefold whole-number products as volumes, e.g., to represent the associative property of multiplication.
description_plain: Your child can find the volume of a rectangular box by filling it with unit cubes and by multiplying its length, width, and height.

subject: Math
description: Relate volume to the operations of multiplication and addition and solve real world and mathematical problems involving volume.
description_plain: Your child understands volume relates to multiplication and addition and can solve problems about volume.

subject: Math
description: Measure volumes by counting unit cubes, using cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft, and improvised units.
description_plain: Your child can measure volume by counting unit cubes using different units like cubic centimeters or cubic inches.

subject: Math
description: A solid figure which can be packed without gaps or overlaps using n unit cubes is said to have a volume of n cubic units.
description_plain: Your child knows that a solid made by fitting together n unit cubes has a volume of n cubic units.

subject: Math
description: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a "unit cube," is said to have "one cubic unit" of volume, and can be used to measure volume.
description_plain: Your child understands a cube with sides one unit long is called a unit cube and has a volume of one cubic unit.

subject: Math
description: Recognize volume as an attribute of solid figures and understand concepts of volume measurement.
description_plain: Your child recognizes volume as a property of solid shapes and understands how to measure it.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of volume and relate volume to multiplication and to addition.
description_plain: Your child understands volume and how it connects to multiplication and addition.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (1/2, 1/4, 1/8). Use operations on fractions for this grade to solve problems involving information presented in line plots.
description_plain: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements with fractions and use fraction math to solve problems from the plot.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Represent and interpret data.

subject: Math
description: Convert among different-sized standard measurement units within a given measurement system (e.g., convert 5 cm to 0.05 m), and use these conversions in solving multi-step, real world problems.
description_plain: Your child can change measurements from one unit to another within the same system and use these changes to solve multi-step problems.

subject: Math
description: Convert like measurement units within a given measurement system.
description_plain: Your child can convert between different units of measurement within the same system.

subject: Math
description: Add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to hundredths, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method and explain the reasoning used.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimals to hundredths using models and strategies, and explain how they solved the problems.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients of whole numbers with up to four-digit dividends and two-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can divide whole numbers with up to four digits by two-digit numbers using place value and other strategies and explain their work.

subject: Math
description: Fluently multiply multi-digit whole numbers using the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can multiply multi-digit whole numbers quickly and accurately using the standard method.

subject: Math
description: Perform operations with multi-digit whole numbers and with decimals to hundredths.

subject: Math

subject: Math

subject: Math
description: Read and write decimals to thousandths using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form, e.g., 347.392 = 3 × 100 + 4 × 10 + 7 × 1 + 3 × (1/10) + 9 × (1/100) + 2 × (1/1000).

subject: Math
description: Read, write, and compare decimals to thousandths.

subject: Math
description: Explain patterns in the number of zeros of the product when multiplying a number by powers of 10, and explain patterns in the placement of the decimal point when a decimal is multiplied or divided by a power of 10. Use whole-number exponents to denote powers of 10.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left.

subject: Math

subject: Math
description: Analyze patterns and relationships.

subject: Math

subject: Language Arts
description: Adapt speech to a variety of contexts and tasks, demonstrating command of 6th grade level language conventions when indicated or appropriate.
description_plain: Your child can change the way they speak depending on the situation and use correct 6th grade language when needed.

subject: Language Arts
description: Include multimedia components and visual displays in presentations to clarify information.
description_plain: Your child can use pictures, videos, or other visuals in presentations to help explain their ideas.

subject: Language Arts
description: Present claims and/or findings, sequencing ideas logically and using pertinent descriptions, facts, and details to accentuate main ideas or themes; use appropriate eye contact, adequate volume, and clear pronunciation.
description_plain: Your child can present their ideas clearly and in order, using facts and details, and speak with good eye contact, volume, and pronunciation.

subject: Language Arts
description: Delineate a speaker's argument and specific claims, distinguishing claims that are supported by reasons and evidence from claims that are not.
description_plain: Your child can explain a speaker's argument and tell which claims are supported by evidence and which are not.

subject: Language Arts
description: Interpret information presented in diverse media and formats and explain how it contributes to a topic, text, or issue under study.
description_plain: Your child can understand information given in different ways, like videos or charts, and explain how it relates to what they are studying.

subject: Language Arts
description: Review the key ideas expressed and demonstrate understanding of multiple perspectives through reflection and paraphrasing.
description_plain: Your child can review and show understanding of different viewpoints by reflecting on and restating what others have said.

subject: Language Arts
description: Pose and respond to specific questions with elaboration and detail by making comments that contribute to the topic, text, or issue under discussion.
description_plain: Your child can ask and answer specific questions with detailed comments that add to the discussion.

subject: Language Arts
description: Follow rules for collegial discussions, set specific goals and deadlines, and define individual roles as needed.
description_plain: Your child can follow discussion rules, set goals and deadlines, and take on roles when needed.

subject: Language Arts
description: Come to discussions prepared, having read or studied required material; explicitly draw on that preparation by referring to evidence on the topic, text, or issue to probe and reflect on ideas under discussion.
description_plain: Your child comes to discussions prepared and uses what they've learned to support their ideas and questions.

subject: Language Arts
description: Engage effectively in a range of collaborative discussions (one-on-one, in groups, and teacher-led) with diverse partners on grade 6 topics, texts, and issues, building on others' ideas and expressing their own clearly.
description_plain: Your child can take part in group discussions, listen to others, build on their ideas, and share their own thoughts clearly.

subject: Language Arts
description: Adapt speech to a variety of contexts and communicative tasks, demonstrating command of formal English when indicated or appropriate.
